course_info.title: Wireframing and Prototyping
course_info.meeting_schedule: Tuesday, Thursday, Friday - 1 hour per session
prerequisites: UX101 - Introduction to User Experience Design

class_schedule.topic: From Sketch to Wireframe: The Fidelity Spectrum
learning_objectives: Differentiate between sketches, wireframes, mockups, and prototypes
Select the appropriate fidelity level for each project phase
Create rapid thumbnail sketches to explore layout alternatives
readings: Sketching User Experiences by Bill Buxton, Ch. 1-3
Prototyping: A Practitioner's Guide by Todd Zaki Warfel, Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Wireframing and prototyping bridge the gap between ideas and implementations. This course builds fluency across the full fidelity spectrum, from napkin sketches to interactive prototypes.
key_concepts: The fidelity spectrum: sketch > wireframe > mockup > prototype
When to use each fidelity: concept testing, layout validation, visual review, interaction testing
Sketching as thinking: externalizing ideas rapidly
The disposable mindset: low-fidelity encourages exploration
Fidelity mismatches: the danger of building hi-fi too early
lecture_notes.demonstration: Drawing 8 different layout approaches for a weather app dashboard in 8 minutes, then discussing trade-offs of each

review.value: Sketch (concept exploration) > Wireframe (layout and structure) > Mockup (visual design) > Prototype (interaction and flow) — each level adds detail and investment
Low-fidelity artifacts should feel cheap to throw away — this encourages exploring more alternatives and prevents emotional attachment to early ideas

learning_objectives: Create structured wireframes using Figma's frame, auto-layout, and component features
Apply wireframe conventions: placeholder images, lorem ipsum, grayscale
Build a reusable wireframe component library for rapid assembly
readings: Wireframing Essentials by Matthew Hamm, Ch. 1-4
Figma documentation: Auto Layout and Components

lecture_notes.introduction: Figma is the industry-standard tool for wireframing and prototyping. Mastering its component and auto-layout systems transforms wireframing from slow pixel-pushing into rapid design assembly.
key_concepts: Wireframe conventions: boxes with X for images, gray rectangles for content blocks
Figma frames and auto-layout for responsive wireframes
Building a wireframe kit: buttons, inputs, cards, navigation, headers
Component variants: different states and sizes from one component
Naming conventions and layer organization for maintainability
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a wireframe component kit from scratch in Figma — creating button, input, card, and navigation components with variants and auto-layout

review.value: Figma feature that makes frames behave like CSS flexbox — items space, align, and resize automatically, essential for responsive wireframes
Single source-of-truth components with multiple states (default, hover, disabled, active) — change the master, every instance updates

learning_objectives: Map complete user flows showing all decision points and paths
Design for edge cases: errors, empty states, loading states
Connect wireframes into coherent flow diagrams
readings: Lean UX by Jeff Gothelf, Ch. on Flow Mapping
Designing for Emotion by Aarron Walter, Ch. on Edge Cases

lecture_notes.introduction: Individual screens mean nothing without the flows that connect them. User flow mapping ensures every path a user might take is designed — including the unhappy paths.
key_concepts: User flow notation: screens, decision diamonds, actions, system responses
Happy path vs. edge cases: both need to be designed
Screen states: default, loading, empty, error, partial, success
Flow complexity management: main flows vs. exception flows
Connecting flows to user stories and acceptance criteria
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mapping the complete checkout flow for an e-commerce app — including payment failure, address validation errors, and session timeout scenarios
setup_requirements: Figma or Miro for flow diagramming

review.value: Every screen has at least 5 states: default (ideal), loading (fetching data), empty (no data yet), error (something failed), partial (some data available) — all must be wireframed
The happy path is the ideal user journey. Edge cases are everything else — errors, interruptions, missing data, unusual inputs. Both need equal wireframing attention.

learning_objectives: Create clickable interactive prototypes using Figma's prototyping features
Implement transitions, overlays, and scroll behaviors for realistic interactions
Design prototype-specific interactions: hover states, component swaps, smart animate
readings: Interaction Design by Dan Saffer, Ch. 3-4

lecture_notes.introduction: Interactive prototypes let users experience a design before any code is written. Figma's prototyping tools can simulate surprisingly complex interactions without engineering.
key_concepts: Hotspot linking: connecting frames with click/tap triggers
Transition types: instant, dissolve, slide, smart animate
Overlays for modals, dropdowns, and contextual menus
Scroll behaviors: vertical scroll, horizontal scroll, fixed positioning
Component interaction: using variants to simulate state changes
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building an interactive prototype of a task management app with navigation, modal dialogs, tab switching, and animated transitions

review.value: Figma automatically animates between two frames when matching layers have changed properties (position, size, opacity) — creates smooth transitions without manual animation
Displaying a frame on top of the current screen (modal, dropdown, tooltip) without navigating away — essential for realistic prototype interactions

class_schedule.topic: Advanced Prototyping: Variables and Conditional Logic
learning_objectives: Use Figma variables to create data-driven prototypes with dynamic content
Implement conditional logic for branching prototype experiences
Prototype complex patterns: form validation, multi-step wizards, personalization
readings: Figma documentation: Variables and Conditionals in Prototyping

lecture_notes.introduction: Advanced prototyping goes beyond click-through screens. With variables and conditional logic, Figma prototypes can simulate real application behavior — dynamic content, form validation, and personalized experiences.
key_concepts: Figma variables: string, number, boolean, and color types
Conditional interactions: if/else logic for branching flows
Simulating form inputs and validation with variable bindings
Multi-step wizard prototypes with progress tracking
Prototyping limitations: when to stop prototyping and start coding
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a multi-step onboarding flow with form validation, conditional paths based on user selections, and a progress bar that updates dynamically

review.value: Named values that persist across frames in a prototype — enabling dynamic text, conditional visibility, counters, and user input simulation without code
If/else logic triggered by variable states — 'if loginAttempts > 3, show lockout screen' — creates realistic branching without duplicating frames

learning_objectives: Plan and conduct moderated prototype usability tests
Identify and classify prototype-specific usability findings
Iterate on wireframes and prototypes based on test findings
readings: Rocket Surgery Made Easy by Steve Krug, Ch. 4-8
Sprint by Jake Knapp, Ch. on Testing

lecture_notes.introduction: The purpose of every prototype is to learn something. Testing prototypes with real users reveals whether your design decisions work — and where they fail — before expensive development begins.
key_concepts: Moderated vs. unmoderated prototype testing: trade-offs
Writing task scenarios for prototype tests
Distinguishing prototype problems from design problems
Capturing findings: severity, frequency, and user impact
Rapid iteration cycles: test, synthesize, redesign, retest
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live moderated usability test of a student prototype, followed by collaborative synthesis and rapid iteration

review.value: Prototype problems are artifacts of the tool (clicking a non-linked area, confusing scroll behavior) — design problems are real usability issues that would persist in the built product. Only design problems should drive iteration.
Test with 3-5 users > synthesize findings same day > redesign top issues > retest with new users — one cycle should take 2-3 days maximum

questions.question: A stakeholder asks you to jump straight to a high-fidelity interactive prototype for a brand new product concept. Explain why this is risky and propose an alternative approach with justification.
questions.answer: Jumping to high-fidelity is risky because: (1) High investment makes the team reluctant to discard or significantly change the design, even when testing reveals problems. (2) Users focus on visual details (colors, fonts) instead of providing feedback on concept and flow. (3) Time spent on visual polish is wasted if the underlying concept or flow is wrong. Alternative approach: Start with paper sketches to explore 3-4 concept directions, validate the best concept with low-fidelity wireframe testing, then build a mid-fidelity interactive prototype for flow testing, and only then move to high-fidelity for visual and interaction refinement. Each fidelity level answers different questions.

rubric.key: Explains how fidelity affects user feedback quality
Proposes staged fidelity approach with rationale

questions.question: List the five essential screen states that every wireframe should account for, and explain why omitting any of them creates user experience problems.
questions.answer: The five screen states: (1) Default — the ideal state with data loaded successfully. (2) Loading — data is being fetched; without this, users think the app is frozen. (3) Empty — no data exists yet; without this, users see a confusing blank screen. (4) Error — something went wrong; without this, users have no feedback and no recovery path. (5) Partial — some data loaded, some failed; without this, users see an incomplete screen with no explanation. Each missing state creates a moment where the user has no idea what is happening or what to do next.

rubric.key: Each state has a clear explanation of its purpose
Explains user impact of missing states

questions.question: Describe how you would prototype a multi-step checkout flow in Figma that includes form validation (showing error messages when fields are empty) and a progress indicator that updates at each step. What Figma features would you use?
questions.answer: Use Figma variables and conditional interactions: (1) Create boolean variables for each required field (e.g., emailFilled, addressFilled). (2) Use component variants for form fields showing default and error states. (3) Set conditional interactions on the 'Next' button: if required variables are false, swap to error-state variants showing error messages; if all are true, navigate to the next step. (4) For the progress indicator, create a component with variants for each step (Step 1, Step 2, Step 3) and swap the variant on each frame transition. (5) Use Smart Animate between steps for smooth progress bar animation.

rubric.key: Correctly describes variable usage for form state
Mentions appropriate Figma features (variants, Smart Animate)

questions.question: During a prototype usability test, a participant clicks on a decorative image expecting it to be interactive, then expresses confusion. Is this a prototype problem or a design problem? How would you determine which it is?
questions.answer: This could be either, and you need to investigate. It is a prototype problem if the image looks non-interactive in the wireframe but the Figma prototype accidentally placed a hotspot over it, or if the testing context primed the user to click everything. It is a design problem if the image has visual affordances that suggest interactivity — such as appearing as a card with hover-like styling, being placed where interactive elements are expected, or looking like a thumbnail that should enlarge. To determine which: (1) Check if the image has visual cues suggesting interactivity. (2) See if multiple participants make the same error (pattern = likely design problem). (3) Ask the participant what they expected to happen. If 2+ of 5 participants click it expecting interaction, treat it as a design problem and either make it interactive or remove the misleading affordance.

rubric.key: Provides concrete criteria for distinguishing them
Mentions pattern analysis across multiple participants

major_assignments.title: Complete Wireframe and Prototype Package
major_assignments.description: Design a complete wireframe and interactive prototype for a mobile app of your choosing. Deliver the full package: user flows, wireframes for all screens and states, an interactive prototype, and usability test findings from testing with at least 3 users.
objectives: Create comprehensive user flow diagrams covering happy paths and edge cases
Wireframe all screens including all five screen states
Build an interactive prototype with realistic transitions and interactions
Test the prototype and iterate based on findings
deliverables: User flow diagrams for at least 3 core user journeys
Wireframes for all screens (minimum 15 unique screens) including all states
Interactive Figma prototype with linked flows and transitions
Usability test plan, session recordings, and findings report
Iteration documentation showing changes made based on test findings

criteria: Flows cover main journeys and key edge cases
Decision points and system responses clearly indicated
All screens in flows are accounted for in wireframes
Flow complexity is appropriate for the app scope
All five screen states addressed for key screens
Consistent wireframe style and component usage
Layout demonstrates strong information hierarchy
Responsive considerations addressed
All flows are interactive and navigable
Transitions are appropriate and consistent
Interactive elements have proper states (hover, active, disabled)
Advanced features used where appropriate (variables, conditionals)
At least 3 users tested with realistic task scenarios
Findings clearly documented with severity ratings
Iterations directly address top usability findings
Before/after comparison shows measurable improvement
Comprehensive user flows covering happy paths and edge cases
High-quality wireframes with all screen states addressed
Polished interactive prototype with realistic interactions
Evidence-based iteration from usability testing with real users

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on wireframing conventions, prototyping techniques, and fidelity decisions

figma_skill_labs.description: Weekly hands-on Figma exercises building specific wireframing and prototyping skills

wireframe_prototype_project.description: Complete wireframe and prototype package for a mobile app with user testing

peer_prototype_review.description: Structured review of peers' prototypes using usability heuristics

final_portfolio.description: Curated portfolio showcasing wireframing and prototyping skills across the semester

course_policies.attendance: Studio sessions require in-person attendance for peer critique and live prototyping workshops. More than 3 absences will reduce your participation and critique grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Figma skill labs are due before each Thursday session. Late labs lose 15% per day and miss peer review. The final project deadline is firm with 10% per day penalty.
course_policies.collaboration: Peer critique is required. Figma component libraries may be shared. All wireframes, prototypes, and deliverables must be individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All wireframes and prototypes must be original work. Using pre-made Figma UI kits is prohibited for graded assignments. Reference and inspiration must be cited.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Figma (free education plan), screen recording software (Loom free tier), and a device for mobile prototype testing (smartphone or tablet).
